title: @DataProvider
overview: A core TestNG feature that allows you to run the exact same test method multiple times with different sets of input data.
whyItMatters: Data-driven testing is essential for scaling automation. Without @DataProvider, you would have to write separate test methods for every possible input combination (e.g., valid login, invalid login, empty login).
explanation:
A method annotated with `@DataProvider` returns a 2D array of Objects (`Object[][]`). Each row in this array represents one execution of the test. The columns represent the parameters passed to the test method.

You link the test to the provider using `@Test(dataProvider = "name")`.

Advanced usage:
- **Cross-class DataProviders**: Store all providers in a separate class and reference them using `dataProviderClass = DataHelper.class`.
- **Parallel Execution**: Add `parallel = true` to the DataProvider annotation to run the data permutations simultaneously.
keyPoints: Parameters in the `@Test` method must exactly match the columns in the `Object[][]`.
Can be configured to run data sets in parallel.
realWorldExample: Testing a search bar. Instead of 50 test methods for 50 different search queries, you write one test method and a DataProvider that feeds it an array of 50 strings. If 2 fail, TestNG reports 48 passed and 2 failed independently.
commonMistakes: Mismatching the number of arguments in the test method with the number of columns in the DataProvider.
Not making the DataProvider method `static` when it is in a different class.
Loading massive datasets into memory all at once instead of using an Iterator.
bestPractices: Keep DataProviders in separate, dedicated data utility classes for better organization.
Combine DataProvider with Apache POI to read data sets directly from Excel files.
Use meaningful names for DataProviders rather than 'dp1' or 'testData'.

interviewQuestions.q: Can a DataProvider return something other than Object[][]?
interviewQuestions.a: Yes, it can return an `Iterator<Object[]>`. This is highly recommended for reading large files (like Excel or CSV) row by row so you don't load the entire file into memory.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you use a DataProvider located in a different class?
interviewQuestions.a: You must specify the class in the Test annotation: `@Test(dataProvider = "name", dataProviderClass = MyData.class)`. Also, the DataProvider method must be declared as static.

revisionSummary: TestNG's @DataProvider is the backbone of data-driven testing in Java. It feeds multidimensional arrays into test methods, allowing a single script to validate countless input scenarios efficiently.
